text: There's a lot to critique in that paper! But what I'd critique most of all is the (clearly admitted) vagueness of the whole concept of AI consciousness.

elements.text: There's a lot to critique in that paper! But what I'd critique most of all is the (clearly admitted) vagueness of the whole concept of AI consciousness.

text: Thanks <@UJBAJNFLK>.  I'll share my work soon.  Mostly needs some diagrams and typing-setting now.  But I go about as deep into the topic as anyone should.  Whether I satisfyingly prove its bottomless and a dead end, will be for readers to decide.

elements.text: .  I'll share my work soon.  Mostly needs some diagrams and typing-setting now.  But I go about as deep into the topic as anyone should.  Whether I satisfyingly prove its bottomless and a dead end, will be for readers to decide.

text: Is anyone talking about how "consciousness", even if it can be defined, is not necessarily the same as having moral rights or ability to suffer? Not in the part of the wired article I can read, at least...

elements.text: Is anyone talking about how "consciousness", even if it can be defined, is not necessarily the same as having moral rights or ability to suffer? Not in the part of the wired article I can read, at least...

text: Yes, talking about it.  And no, the article is incomplete.  In my letter, I quote Scott Alexander commenting on the same AI consciousness research.  <https://www.astralcodexten.com/p/the-new-ai-consciousness-paper>

elements.text: Yes, talking about it.  And no, the article is incomplete.  In my letter, I quote Scott Alexander commenting on the same AI consciousness research.

text: To tl;dr <@U016VUZGUUQ>, where Pollan says, "What would it mean for humanity to discover one day in the not‑so‑distant future that a fully conscious machine had come into the world? I’m guessing it would be a Copernican moment, abruptly dislodging our sense of centrality and specialness."  Alexander and I suspect it would be similar to the moment when someone first discovers that pork comes from pigs.

elements.text: , where Pollan says, "What would it mean for humanity to discover one day in the not‑so‑distant future that a fully conscious machine had come into the world? I’m guessing it would be a Copernican moment, abruptly dislodging our sense of centrality and specialness."  Alexander and I suspect it would be similar to the moment when someone first discovers that pork comes from pigs.

text: Or maybe Wilber learning what farmed pigs are for.  Anyhow, in my own experience, the not‑so‑distant future was roughly yesterday.

elements.text: Or maybe Wilber learning what farmed pigs are for.  Anyhow, in my own experience, the not‑so‑distant future was roughly yesterday.

text: The SA article and Pollan quote are both interesting, but neither really address my question at all. Certainly not directly.

elements.text: The SA article and Pollan quote are both interesting, but neither really address my question at all. Certainly not directly.

text: Fair.  Let's see.  I have about 3 million words of interesting AI stuff from the last three years indexed on my laptop here.  Here are three relevant links: <https://mustafa-suleyman.ai/seemingly-conscious-ai-is-coming>, <https://youtube.com/watch?v=I9aGC6Ui3eE>, <https://thoughtforms.life/qa-from-the-internet-and-recent-presentations-3/>.  And if you want quotes, it's only a little effort on my part to produce them.
